pattern: At most two values can each appear more than a third of the time, so a Boyer-Moore vote with two slots finds the only candidates in one pass and a verification pass confirms their counts.
hints: How many distinct values can each occur strictly more than n/3 times? The answer is at most two, which bounds how many candidates you ever need to track.
Generalize the single-candidate majority vote: keep two candidate slots with two counters, cancelling a vote only when an incoming value matches neither candidate and both counters are positive.
After the voting pass the two survivors are only candidates, so make a second pass counting their true occurrences and keep those exceeding n/3.

edge_inputs.description: one value repeats past a third, the other appears once
single element is trivially over a third
two distinct elements, neither exceeds a third of length two
two values each appear exactly n/3 times, so neither qualifies (strict threshold)
two genuine winners must both be reported
all identical elements yield a single winner
negative values exercise the same counting logic

stress.description: random small-alphabet array where winners arise by chance and are verified by the count pass
stress.note: small value range makes occurrences over n/3 common so both branches are exercised

stress.description: constructed array with exactly two values planted above a third plus filler noise
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(6, size)
    a, b = rng.sample(range(-1000, 1000), 2)
    cnt = n // 3 + 1
    arr = [a] * cnt + [b] * cnt
    while len(arr) < n:
        arr.append(rng.randint(-1000000, 1000000))
    rng.shuffle(arr)
    return [arr]
stress.note: guarantees two winners exist so the multi-candidate path is covered
